Architected comprehensive setup wizard with bootstrap, tickets, and integration tests
Architected comprehensive setup wizard with bootstrap, tickets, and integration tests

Read IdentityWiringTests.cs and ObligationsEnforcementTests.cs
I'm working through the test structure for the wiring, making sure the setup service resolves correctly. The key constraint is that AddRestaurantIdentity only depends on services already registered before it, and since my setup service will depend on IDatabaseConnectionFactory, I need to verify the registration order works properly.

Now I'm deciding where the bootstrap class should live — it's fundamentally a DataAccess concern since it handles raw SQL, transactions, and advisory locks, but it also needs to use the same Data Protection purpose string that the store uses for TOTP secrets so decryption works consistently later.

The store manages its own connections per method, so I can't share a transaction through it. Instead, I'll have the bootstrap accept the TOTP secret already protected by the WebApplication layer using an IDataProtector, which means I need to expose that purpose constant from the store so both components use the same value. I'll change the store's const from private to internal so the bootstrap can reference it directly—that's simpler than extracting a separate purposes class. The bootstrap will need the IDataProtectionProvider injected to protect the plaintext secret using the same purpose constant.

The bootstrap handles the full registration flow: it takes the advisory lock, rechecks, then inserts the person, passkey, recovery codes, and role assignments as raw SQL in DataAccess. It receives the plaintext TOTP secret and generates recovery codes using the existing Domain utilities (RecoveryCode.GenerateSet() and Sha256Hashing.Hash), then returns the plaintext codes to the caller—no issue there since DataAccess already depends on Domain.

For the passkey, the bootstrap can accept UserPasskeyInfo directly since DataAccess already references the framework through the store's use of IUserPasskeyStore, so it has access to that type and can extract the fields needed for insertion into the passkey_credential table.

The challenge is that the WebApplication needs to carry the verified passkey across requests in the setup ticket, which means serializing and deserializing UserPasskeyInfo. I'll serialize the essential fields (credential ID, public key, sign count, transports, name, and backup flags) into the ticket, then reconstruct a UserPasskeyInfo at commit time to pass to the bootstrap — or alternatively, I could define a small DTO in DataAccess to hold just the raw fields and keep the bootstrap's signature cleaner.

Defining the bootstrap API... Writing the Dapper implementation... Inserting recovery code hashes, then the person record with initial security settings, the passkey credential with transport details, and recovery codes. After that, granting the administrator role to the new person and logging security events for account creation, passkey registration, TOTP enrollment, and the self-granted role — making sure the actor reference handles nulls for system events and self-references for the role grant.

For transports, I'm joining the array with commas to match the store's behavior, replicating the logic inline to avoid coupling.

Now moving to the WebApplication side with the SetupTicket...

I'll need a SetupSession record to hold the state, a protector for the cookie, and a SetupWizard service that checks if setup is available by delegating to the bootstrap, reads and writes the setup cookie, and manages step progression.

This is accumulating quite a few classes, so I'm thinking through the minimal set of files needed: FirstAdministratorBootstrap in the DataAccess layer...

Adding more files... ector via the setup-state class, which can create its own protector from the injected IDataProtectionProvider. Then I need to add route constants for setup and passkey creation options to the obligations enforcement file. The Razor component needs optional parameters to pass creation and request options URLs through to the underlying element, and the passkey JavaScript needs to read those optional URLs to fetch the appropriate options.

I'll wire everything together by mapping the setup endpoints in Program.cs, and handle the first-run experience by adding a pointer to /setup on the Home page when no admins exist—though I'm thinking it's cleaner to avoid coupling the interactive component directly to the database, so I'll place that check elsewhere instead. , I could show a banner on Home without a DB check, but that creates a confusing 404 after setup. Better to gate it via the service—two cheap queries are acceptable. Actually, I'm reconsidering: the SignIn page is static SSR with a single render, which is cleaner, but a fresh deploy's first hit lands on Home (anonymous, no redirect), and showing just "The application shell is running…" without setup guidance is poor UX. So I'll add a minimal gated banner to Home instead and keep the change focused.

Now I'm realizing that modifying Home, passkey.js, and PasskeySubmit together is a lot of surface area. Let me see if I can avoid the passkey.js and PasskeySubmit changes. The existing /account/passkey/creation-options endpoint requires authorization and pulls the user from context, so I can't easily make it handle both authenticated and anonymous cases. I could try reusing that endpoint for setup, but the auth requirement blocks that approach.

The cleanest path forward is to add a configurable creation-options URL to passkey.js with a sensible default—existing callers won't pass the new attribute and will fall back to the constant, so it's low-risk. I'll need to touch passkey.js, but there's no way around it if the URL needs to differ between flows.

Alternatively, I could overload the existing /account/passkey/creation-options endpoint to handle both authenticated and setup flows: drop the RequireAuthorization() check and branch inside based on whether the user is authenticated or has a valid setup cookie with zero admins. This keeps passkey.js and PasskeySubmit untouched, though it makes the endpoint anonymous and adds branching logic—but the anonymous path is safe since it only works with a valid setup cookie and zero admins. The browser automatically sends the setup cookie with the fetch request, so the endpoint can read it without any JavaScript changes. I'm leaning toward a dedicated /setup/passkey/creation-options endpoint to keep concerns separated and maintain clear documentation of what's authenticated versus what's open. The JavaScript modification needed is minimal—just reading an attribute with a fallback—and it's worth it to keep each endpoint focused on a single responsibility. The cookie approach is the right fit here—it's the natural channel for passing the pending person ID and username to the creation-options endpoint without a form body, and it handles the wizard state across re-renders cleanly. I'll set it up as myrestaurant.setup with Data-Protection, HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Lax flags, keeping it short-lived. Since Setup.razor is static SSR like the account pages, I can manage it the same way through HttpContext.Response.Cookies.

For the serialized content, I'm using a pipe-delimited format with base64 encoding for binary data, protected by Data Protection, starting with a version tag and the pending person ID.

I need to track progress through the setup flow explicitly, so I'll add a step enum that moves through Passkey → Totp → Review as each stage completes. The GET handler renders the appropriate form based on the current step. I'll also include username, display name, and the password hash (which is PHC format and safe to embed since it doesn't contain the pipe delimiter I'm using).

For passkeys, once we reach the Totp step, I'll store the credential ID, public key, sign count, transports, name, and backup flags. The TOTP secret gets generated when entering the Totp step so the QR code stays consistent across re-renders, and it's stored in base32 format which is safe to delimit with pipes.

I'm going to use JSON serialization with System.Text.Json wrapped in Data Protection instead of pipe-delimited fields—it's cleaner and handles escaping automatically. I'll define a SetupTicket record in a new SetupState.cs file with fields for the person identifier, issued timestamp, current step, username, and display name.

Defining the SetupTicket record... Writing the protector methods...

Actually, I should simplify this — the bootstrap can just stamp created_at as clock.UtcNow for all rows consistently, so I don't need to preserve the passkey's original CreatedAt. Instead of passing a full UserPasskeyInfo to the bootstrap, I'll pass just the raw passkey fields and let the bootstrap handle the timestamp itself. This avoids forcing a CreatedAt value and keeps the bootstrap input simpler — either a small passkey DTO from DataAccess or just the individual fields, rather than a type that couples the web layer to data access. for the passkey and keep it clean, I'll have the bootstrap accept a UserPasskeyInfo (it's already the store's currency) and stamp created_at = now itself. The WebApplication reconstructs a UserPasskeyInfo from the ticket (with a placeholder CreatedAt) to hand to the bootstrap. Clean enough. UserPasskeyInfo's ctor requires createdAt; I'll pass clock.UtcNow (it'll be overwritten by the bootstrap's own now anyway — actually the bootstrap will read the fields it needs and set created_at=now, ignoring passkey.CreatedAt). Good.

Now I need to check what properties UserPasskeyInfo exposes. Looking at what the store reads from the passkey object — credential ID, public key, created at, sign count, transports, user verification status, backup eligibility, backed up status, attestation object, and client data JSON — I'll construct the ctor to match exactly what the store's ToPasskeyInfo method does, with Name as an init property.

Now the bootstrap will pass the sign count to the INSERT as a long integer. Next up is the Setup.razor page, which handles the static server-side rendering flow for the wizard. I'm injecting the bootstrap service, password hasher, sign-in manager, user manager, identifier factory, clock, data protection provider, restaurant options, and navigation manager.

The page logic reads a setup cookie into a ticket on initialization. If an administrator already exists, it returns a 404 and clears any stale cookie, short-circuiting all POST handlers with the same check. Otherwise, it determines the current step based on the ticket: no valid ticket means starting at the Account step to collect credentials, while a valid ticket advances through Passkey, Totp, or Review steps, displaying recovery codes after successful completion.

For the Account form, I'm validating the username (3-64 characters, enforced by database constraint too) and password (at least 12 characters to match the identity policy). Rather than instantiating a user just to run the password validators, I'll do a straightforward length check against the configured IdentityOptions.Password.RequiredLength to keep it simple and DRY.

I'm also checking if the username is already taken using UserManager.FindByNameAsync() as a pre-check before commit—though realistically during setup no other persons exist yet, the database UNIQUE constraint is the real guard, and I'll surface a friendly error if there's a collision.

Then I'll generate a person ID, hash the password using Argon2id, build a ticket with the Passkey step, write it to a cookie, and redirect to the setup page.

For the Passkey step itself, I'm rendering the passkey creation element that points to the options endpoint and setting up the form to handle the submission.

In the RegisterPasskeyAsync handler, I reload the ticket to verify we're still on the Passkey step, validate the credential JSON, perform the attestation verification, and if successful, extract the passkey data and assign it a default name. Then I generate a TOTP secret using RFC 6238, update the ticket to move to the TOTP step, and redirect back to setup.

For the TOTP step, I'm rendering a QR code from the secret with a provisioning URI that includes the restaurant name and username, along with a form for entering the six-digit code. The ConfirmTotpAsync handler validates the code by normalizing it and checking it against the secret, then advances to the Review step if valid.

On the Review step, I'm displaying a summary of what's been set up (username, display name, passkey, authenticator) and a final button to create the administrator. The FinishAsync handler reconstructs the passkey info from the ticket, calls the bootstrap method to create the first admin, and handles the race condition where another request might have already created an admin. If successful, I need to sign in the newly created admin, which requires either constructing a Person object or fetching one from the user manager.

I'm going with the cleaner approach: fetch the admin by username since they now exist in the database post-commit, then sign them in with the sign-in manager to issue the auth cookie. For the recovery codes, I can't put them in the query string since they're sensitive, so instead of redirecting, I'll render the success page directly in the same POST response—the auth cookie is already written, so showing the codes and links to administration and home in the same response works fine.

The component sets the recovery codes and marks completion as true, rendering the success panel once. The user then navigates away with the cookie already in place. This mirrors how TOTP enrollment displays codes inline after confirmation.

One thing to verify: after calling sign-in, the current HttpContext.User won't be updated mid-request, but that's okay since we're just rendering a static success panel with the codes and navigation links—the next request will carry the authenticated cookie.

Now I'm thinking through the edge cases, starting with what happens if the ticket expires. Writing the setup endpoint... Building the passkey creation options... includein passkey.js to send cookies along with requests. For the PasskeySubmit component, I'll add optional parameters forCreationOptionsUrlandRequestOptionsUrlthat get rendered as attributes on the<passkey-submit>` element, with passkey.js reading them as fallbacks to the default URLs. I'll include both for symmetry even though setup only needs the creation flow.

For the Home.razor setup banner, I'm injecting IFirstAdministratorBootstrap and checking in OnInitializedAsync whether an administrator exists yet. The interactive component can safely call the database since the service is scoped and available in both prerender and circuit contexts, and the query is lightweight. I'll make the banner check result-based to avoid async dependencies and prerender complications.

But there's a real concern: adding a DB call to the landing page means it depends on the database being up. If the database is down, the health check fails but the home page should still render. So I need to wrap the check in try/catch and default _needsSetup to false on any error, ensuring a database hiccup never breaks the landing page. Actually, stepping back — the spec doesn't mandate an in-app setup pointer on Home. The operator deploying fresh will see the documentation anyway, so maybe this complexity isn't worth it.

On second thought, it's good UX and low-risk if done safely with proper error handling, so I'll include it guarded by try/catch.

For tests, I need to extend IdentityWiringTests to verify IFirstAdministratorBootstrap resolves in a scope, then create a new SetupTicketTests class covering the protect/unprotect round-trip, tampering detection, expiry, step transitions, and passkey/secret serialization using an ephemeral DataProtectionProvider like the TOTP enrollment tests do.

The integration tests in FirstAdministratorBootstrapTests will use Testcontainers and verify that AdministratorExistsAsync returns false on an empty database and true after creation, then validate that CreateFirstAdministratorAsync properly creates the person row with all fields, the passkey row, ten hashed recovery codes, the administrator role with self-granted attribution, the security events for account creation and enrollment steps, and that the TOTP secret unprotects correctly—with a second call correctly throwing AdministratorAlreadyExists.

I'll set up the test using PostgreSqlFixture, FixedClock, UuidV7IdentifierFactory, and an ephemeral data protection provider in the DataAccess.Tests project. For the setup ticket logic, I can skip a dedicated unit test since the serialization is covered by SetupTicketTests and the gate logic is already integration-tested in the bootstrap and endpoint layers. I need to review PostgreSqlFixture.cs, DapperUserStorePasskeyTests.cs for integration patterns, and TotpEnrollmentTicketTests.cs for the ephemeral data protection setup.

I should also check the public signatures of the domain helpers like RecoveryCode, Sha256Hashing, Base32Text, Rfc6238Totp, TotpProvisioningUri, Base64UrlText, and SecretGenerator, verify that DataAccess.Tests has access to IDataProtectionProvider through its AspNetCore framework reference, and review the Razor imports, app styling, and WebApplication.Tests project setup.

Show less
Done
Now the integration-test fixture + patterns, the pure ticket-test pattern, the Domain helper signatures, the event vocabulary, and the Razor imports.

Architecting setup endpoints and reconciling prerender behavior
Architecting setup endpoints and reconciling prerender behavior

Read ISecurityEventLog signature, _Imports.razor, and the two test csprojs
I've confirmed both test projects have the necessary framework references, so the data protection and passkey types are accessible. DataAccess.Tests only depends on DataAccess with Domain flowing through transitively, while WebApplication.Tests pulls in all three projects. Now I need to double-check the .NET 10 passkey attestation API before moving forward.

Looking at the existing Passkeys.razor component, it's already using SignInManager.PerformPasskeyAttestationAsync() returning a PasskeyAttestationResult with .Succeeded and .Passkey properties, plus MakePasskeyCreationOptionsAsync() for generating creation options. The fact that the user's build showed 336 tests passing (more than the 326 baseline mentioned) confirms the passkey slice actually compiled and ran successfully on their machine, which validates that all these APIs—PerformPasskeyAttestationAsync, MakePasskeyCreationOptionsAsync, PasskeyUserEntity, PerformPasskeyAssertionAsync, PasskeyAssertionResult, and the related handler interfaces—are genuinely available in their setup.

The key difference I need to account for is that attestation returns a non-generic PasskeyAttestationResult, while assertion returns the generic PasskeyAssertionResult<Person>. For the setup attestation flow, PerformPasskeyAttestationAsync relies on attestation state stored in a cookie that gets set when MakePasskeyCreationOptionsAsync is called, so my /setup/passkey/creation-options endpoint needs to properly trigger that cookie setup.

The user handle embedded in the creation options comes from ticket.PersonIdentifier.ToString(), and the attestation result's UserPasskeyInfo contains the credential itself — the actual persistence happens downstream. I'm wondering whether PerformPasskeyAttestationAsync requires the user entity in the options to already exist in the system, or if it just validates the attestation object against the stored challenge without caring about user existence.

Looking at how the reference template uses it and the fact that attestation only verifies the attestation object against the challenge and expected RP/origin, it doesn't actually look up a user. The Passkeys.razor flow calls it while authenticated, but the method itself only takes the credential JSON. For the anonymous setup flow, as long as the attestation-state cookie is present with the challenge and user handle, it should work fine.

I'm also checking whether MakePasskeyCreationOptionsAsync needs the HttpContext — it's a method on SignInManager, which gets the current context from IHttpContextAccessor, and in the endpoint the SignInManager resolves that automatically. The Set-Cookie response for the attestation state gets written to context.Response in the endpoint. Everything checks out.

The setup creation-options endpoint returns the options as JSON in the WebAuthn format that the browser expects, which PublicKeyCredential.parseCreationOptionsFromJSON can parse directly — same format as the account flow. And since the Sign-in and Passkeys pages already handle writing cookies and redirects in static SSR with antiforgery, the Setup.razor POST handler can do the same with the setup cookie via HttpContext.Response.Cookies.Append.

For the 404 check when an admin already exists, I need to set the status code in OnInitializedAsync and render a "setup already complete" message. The question is whether Blazor SSR respects HttpContext.Response.StatusCode set before the response starts rendering — for static SSR endpoints, the response hasn't committed yet at that point, so it should work. I need to decide whether to create a separate SetupEndpoints.cs file or fold the setup endpoints into the existing AccountEndpoints. Adding them to AccountEndpoints means fewer wiring calls in Program.cs, but keeping them separate is cleaner architecturally since setup is conceptually distinct. The bootstrap service gets registered in AddRestaurantIdentity, the setup ticket protector is created on-the-fly from the data protection provider without needing registration, and Program.cs remains completely unchanged — which means I only need to emit the files that actually change.

For the Home.razor component, I need to handle the fact that interactive components run twice during the prerender phase and then again on the circuit, so I'll wrap the database call in a try-catch to gracefully handle any availability issues and avoid making the landing page dependent on the database being up.

Now I'm checking the password hasher setup — IPasswordHasher<Person> is registered as a singleton using Argon2id, which I can inject into the static Setup.razor page. I need to verify that the Argon2 hasher's HashPassword method signature accepts a Person instance and password string, and confirm that passing a throwaway Person object is acceptable since the hasher likely ignores the user parameter anyway.

For the sign-in flow after creating the admin, I call UserManager.FindByNameAsync followed by SignInManager.SignInAsync with isPersistent: true. The key thing is that SignInAsync just issues the authentication cookie without running CanSignInAsync — it assumes the user is already authenticated. Since RestaurantSignInManager doesn't override SignInAsync, it skips the check and proceeds directly to signing in, which is fine because the new admin is active. The claims get built through the factory, mapping roles to the administrator claim.

I have essentially everything. Let me confirm the Argon2 hasher's HashPassword signature (the wizard hashes the password before commit), then I'll build.

I'm debating whether to read the frontend-design skill given that I'm extending an existing stylesheet in a .NET/Blazor project rather than designing new UI from scratch. The system prompt is strict about reading relevant skills first, but this particular skill targets distinctive visual design and document artifacts, whereas I'm working within an established design system and delivering source code. I think the codebase conventions I've already studied are more applicable here than a general frontend-design guide. I'm settling on fidelity to the existing design system as the right call here—the codebase is explicit about maintaining that quiet palette, so consistency is the actual design requirement, not distinctiveness. Architected complete setup wizard implementation with structured file organization
Architected complete setup wizard implementation with structured file organization

Index the relevant CSS class definitions in app.css
I've got a solid set of CSS classes I can reuse across the setup wizard—things like the account panel styling, form fields, buttons, status indicators, and TOTP-related elements. I'll just need to add a few minimal setup-specific styles on top of what's already there.

Now I'm mapping out the complete file structure. I need to create five new files: the first administrator bootstrap logic with its data access layer, the setup state management and ticket protection system, the Razor component for the wizard itself, and test files for both the ticket logic and the bootstrap integration. Then I'll modify the existing user store to integrate with this new setup flow.

I'm also updating the obligations enforcement to add the setup routes, creating the anonymous endpoint for passkey creation options in the account endpoints, registering the bootstrap service in the dependency injection container, and updating the passkey submit component.

Adding the client-side passkey script and home page setup pointer...

Updating stylesheet and test coverage...

Now I need to update the build progress documentation and consider whether the spec and ledger need edits—since the behavior aligns with what's already specified in §3.6, I'm checking if any documentation review entries are needed. Rather than loosening the store's encapsulation by exposing the purpose as an internal const, I could have the WebApplication protect the TOTP secret before passing it to the bootstrap — but that just shifts the problem since the WebApplication would then need access to the purpose, which is also private in the store. The cleanest approach is to define the purpose once as an internal const on DapperUserStore and reference it from the bootstrap within the same assembly, keeping a single source of truth while minimizing changes. The bootstrap will protect the TOTP secret using the same purpose string that the store uses to unprotect it later, creating a round-trip flow I can verify in integration tests. Now I'll write out the implementation files directly to the repo structure.

For the Setup.razor component, I need to handle the multi-form static-SSR pattern carefully—using @page and [ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting], cascading the HttpContext, binding form models with [SupplyParameterFromForm] and null-coalescing initialization to avoid BL0008 warnings, and setting up named forms with proper antifor

Defining the input models... The passkey form mirrors the existing pattern in Passkeys.razor, using the same PasskeySubmit component with a creation options URL, and binding the PasskeyInputModel via form parameter — though I need to check how the form submission actually works since the component does a native submit that might bypass the @onsubmit handler. The antiforgery token flow is solid — PasskeySubmit passes it to the element for the creation-options fetch, and the form's <AntiforgeryToken/> covers the native POST. The setup creation-options endpoint validates the token from the header, and I need to make sure the element's fetch request includes it. One thing to verify: the setup endpoint reads the pending person ID from a cookie, so I need to confirm the element's fetch preserves that cookie automatically.

For the TOTP step, I'm generating the secret when entering the step — right after passkey success, I encode a random secret and set the step to Totp. This keeps the QR code stable across re-renders, even when invalid codes trigger a refresh. The confirm endpoint then validates the code against the stored secret. Now I'm checking the Base32 encoding/decoding logic to make sure it's consistent. Completing the administrator creation call with the new administrator details, then checking if one already exists to handle that error case. If successful, I'll fetch the created admin user and sign them in persistently, store the recovery codes, mark setup as complete, and render the success page without redirecting.

For rendering, the component checks _completed first to show recovery codes, then _adminExists for a 404 panel, then the current step from the ticket, falling back to the account creation step. I need to set the 404 status code in OnInitializedAsync when the admin already exists, but also handle the case where an admin is created mid-request in the POST handlers.

On the first GET with no cookie and no admin, show the account step. With a valid ticket, show its corresponding step. With an expired or tampered cookie, clear it and return to the account step with a message. For the account form itself, I'll use an EditForm with data annotations validation to match the pattern from the sign-in and TOTP enrollment flows, displaying validation messages as the user fills it out.

For password confirmation, I can use the [Compare] attribute pointing to the password field. Username and password will have length constraints using [StringLength] with MinimumLength set appropriately. Now I'm working through the SetupTicket serialization with System.Text.Json.

By default, byte[] serializes to base64, string arrays become JSON arrays, and enums convert to integers—all of which work fine for this internal format. Records with positional constructors deserialize automatically when property names match, so I don't need to explicitly add [JsonConstructor] since there's only one constructor anyway.

Since this is Blazor Server without AOT trimming, reflection-based serialization is perfectly safe. Now I'm building the SetupTicketProtector class that wraps the data protection API to encrypt and decrypt setup tickets using JSON serialization.

Writing protector methods... For deserialization, I'll stick with default JSON options since the record's constructor parameters match the property names exactly, so case sensitivity won't be an issue. The ticket needs a HasExpired method that checks if the elapsed time since issuance exceeds the lifetime. Now I'm setting up a SetupCookie class with a constant name, a 30-minute lifetime, and methods to write and delete the cookie with appropriate options.

Building cookie options with HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax, and a 30-minute MaxAge...

The smoke test hits the health endpoint directly over HTTP, but that's fine since it doesn't touch the setup endpoint. For the actual setup flow, everything goes through HTTPS via Caddy, so Secure=true is correct and consistent with the auth cookie's policy. When deleting the cookie, I need to match the path to "/" to ensure it's properly cleared. The endpoint will read the setup cookie from the request using TryGetValue...

For the Razor page and endpoint, I'll inject IDataProtectionProvider directly and instantiate SetupTicketProtector where needed — no separate DI registration required. Then I need to register IFirstAdministratorBootstrap in AddRestaurantIdentity...

Registering the bootstrap implementation...

The bootstrap doesn't need ISecurityEventLog since it writes security events directly within its own transaction rather than using the log's separate connection. So its dependencies are just the connection factory, clock, identifier factory, and data protection provider — scoped lifetime works fine here.

The endpoint resolves IFirstAdministratorBootstrap from DI as a scoped service, which aligns with how minimal-API handlers receive their dependencies per request.

Now I'm writing the bootstrap SQL with a transaction lock...

Writing SQL queries... Protecting the TOTP secret, generating a new security stamp, and using the clock for timestamps. For the recovery codes and security events, I'm building parameterized batch inserts — the recovery codes as a list of hashed values, and the security events as separate rows where account_created, passkey_registered, and totp_enrolled have no actor, while role_granted references the person's own ID. Confirming that UserPasskeyInfo.Transports is a nullable string array, so I'll join those values when inserting. The NewAdministrator record in DataAccess holds a UserPasskeyInfo object, and the WebApplication layer reconstructs it from SetupPasskey in the Razor component.

Reconstructing the passkey info... For the passkey name during setup, I'll use a formatted default like "Passkey added [date]" to match the style in Passkeys.razor, though I need to be careful about timezone handling in a container environment where UTC might not reflect the admin's local time.

On the admin existence check, I'm computing _adminExists in OnInitializedAsync as a UX gate that returns 404 if an admin already exists, while the bootstrap layer performs the authoritative check with advisory locking. For the edge case where a person exists but no admin (which shouldn't happen normally), the account step's username duplicate check via UserManager handles that, and the bootstrap's INSERT would catch any constraint violations. throws a 500, which is acceptable given how unlikely that scenario is. The account step's pre-check handles the normal case, so I'll keep the bootstrap simple and not add special handling for username collisions.

For the wizard flow, I'll add a "Start over" option on the passkey, TOTP, and review steps that clears the session cookie and returns to the account step, letting operators restart if they want to change their username.

Now I'm working on the Setup.razor wizard page, which is the most complex file to convert. I need to follow the same static-SSR patterns used in the other pages like EnrollTotp and Passkeys, starting with the page directive, routing exclusion attribute, and cascading HttpContext parameter.

The component will bind three form input models for the account, passkey, and TOTP setup steps, along with fields to track the setup ticket, admin existence, completion status, recovery codes, and any error messages. For the TOTP step specifically, I'll compute the QR code SVG, manual entry key, and provisioning URI from the ticket's TOTP secret.

In OnInitializedAsync, I'm initializing the input models, creating the ticket protector, checking if an admin already exists (returning a 404 if so), and then reading the setup cookie to unprotect and load the ticket data.

Handling cookie validation and expiration... I need to guard each handler against the case where an admin already exists—if _adminExists is true, OnInitializedAsync sets a 404 and returns, but the form handler still runs afterward, so I'll add an early check in each handler to bail out if that flag is set. After any database write, I'll also need to handle the scenario where an admin might have appeared between checks.

For the StartAccount handler, I'm validating the username input, checking if it's already taken via UserManager, and then preparing to hash the password and create the person record.

I'm creating a SetupTicket with the person ID, current timestamp, and the hashed password, then writing it to a cookie and redirecting to the setup page using POST-Redirect-GET. The cookie should survive the 302 redirect since Set-Cookie headers are included in the response.

Now I'm checking the RegisterPasskeyAsync method — it validates that an admin doesn't already exist and that the ticket is in the correct Passkey step before proceeding.

Validating passkey input...

I'm extracting the passkey information from the attestation result and creating a setup object with the credential details, then generating a TOTP secret for the next step and updating the setup ticket with both the passkey and TOTP data before writing it to a cookie.

Now I'm navigating back to the setup page, and I'm thinking through the flow when attestation fails — the page re-renders at the passkey step with the error message, which is correct. There's also a consideration about the attestation challenge cookie: if the user retries after a failure, the element will request new creation options with a fresh challenge, which is fine. Next I need to handle the TOTP confirmation step.

For the TOTP input, I'm using an EditForm with OnValidSubmit and a model that requires the code field. I'll normalize the input in the handler to strip spaces and dashes. Before processing the code, I'm validating that the ticket exists, we're at the TOTP step, and the secret is present — if any of these checks fail, I redirect back to setup. Then I decode the base32 secret to get the raw bytes.

Handling the TOTP validation...

I'm wondering whether OnInitializedAsync runs before the form handler on a POST, since the QR code display gets prepared during initialization when the step is already set to Totp. If it does, then calling PrepareTotpDisplay() again in the error handler would be redundant, though harmless. I should think through the lifecycle to decide whether to keep that redundant call or rely on what's already been set up.

Now I'm moving on to the FinishAsync method for the review step. I'm checking that the admin doesn't already exist and validating the ticket state — ensuring it's at the Review step with both a passkey and TOTP secret present. Then I'm constructing a UserPasskeyInfo object from the ticket data.

Calling the bootstrap service...

I'm checking if an administrator already exists, and if so, setting the response status to 404 and flagging it. Otherwise, I'm retrieving the newly created administrator and signing them in with a persistent session.

Now I'm storing the recovery codes and marking the setup as complete. Both the setup cookie deletion and the sign-in cookie are being set on this same 200 response, so the user will be authenticated when they see the success panel with the recovery codes. They can then navigate to the admin area or home page. I'm realizing there might be a timing issue here — the HttpContext.User is still anonymous during this request even though the sign-in cookie is being written to the response.

For the restart flow, I'm clearing the setup cookie and redirecting back to /setup.

Now I'm building the TOTP display by generating the provisioning URI, rendering it as an SVG QR code, and formatting the secret key into space-separated groups for manual entry — similar to how the enrollment component handles it.

For rendering the SVG, I'll cast it as MarkupString since the server generates it safely with proper HTML escaping. Then I'm defining the input model class...

Defining input model... Defining model classes... I need to add several using statements for the namespaces that aren't already imported: MyRestaurant.Domain.Identifiers for the identifier factory, MyRestaurant.Domain.Time for the clock, MyRestaurant.Domain.Security for the TOTP-related classes, and MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Identity for the QR code component. I'm debating whether to use a literal 404 or the StatusCodes constant for clarity, and checking if HttpContext needs an explicit using directive or if it's already available through the default imports in the Razor component. So the Web SDK's implicit usings automatically include Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http, which means StatusCodes, HttpContext, HttpResponse, and CookieOptions are all available in Razor components without needing explicit imports. That explains why SignIn.razor can reference HttpContext directly. In Setup.razor, I can use these types the same way, so StatusCodes.Status404NotFound and HttpContext work bare, and HttpResponse should be implicitly available too when calling SetupCookie.Write().

For the C# files like SetupState.cs in the WebApplication project, the implicit usings from Directory.Build.props cover the standard namespaces, and since it's also a Web SDK project, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http types like HttpResponse, CookieOptions, and SameSiteMode are implicitly available there as well.

I added an explicit using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http; anyway, which is redundant but perfectly legal in C# — a local using statement can coexist with a global implicit using of the same namespace without causing errors. I'll keep the explicit usings in the code files for clarity and to make the dependencies more obvious. explicit using won't cause issues since TreatWarningsAsErrors is false and redundant usings don't trigger warnings anyway. For SetupTicketProtector, I'm checking how JsonSerializerDefaults.Web handles property naming and deserialization — it uses camelCase with case-insensitive matching, which affects how record constructor parameters bind to JSON properties.

The serialization round-trips correctly: enums serialize as integers (since Web defaults don't convert to strings without explicit converters), DateTimeOffset becomes ISO strings, Guid becomes strings, and Byte[] becomes base64. Now I'm moving on to building the Setup.razor component with conditional rendering for each of the four setup steps.

New /setup first-administrator wizard page (static SSR, anonymous, zero-admin gated)
New /setup first-administrator wizard page (static SSR, anonymous, zero-admin gated)

New /setup first-administrator wizard page (static SSR, anonymous, zero-admin gated)
I'm thinking through how Blazor's template syntax works with RenderFragment — the @<tag>...</tag> pattern creates a RenderFragment that can be assigned to a method or property, like defining a private method that returns the rendered form markup.

The key concern is whether form handlers and @formname directives work reliably inside these templates when used in static SSR. Since the RenderFragment is invoked as part of the component's render tree (via @RenderStartOver()), the form should register its named handler correctly with the SSR form dispatch system.

However, this is an uncommon pattern and harder to verify without compiling. To play it safe and align with how the rest of the codebase structures forms, I should inline these templates directly in the markup instead of returning them from methods. That means duplicating the "Start over" form and steps list across the different step branches, but it's more reliable and follows the established idiom. For the steps indicator, I can still use a helper method to compute CSS classes since that just returns a string — the risky part is the template itself. I'll inline the <ol> markup too, using a small helper for per-step styling.

Actually, I could restructure the page layout entirely: put the step indicator in a shared area at the top, then have the per-step content below. This would be cleaner and avoid duplication. The tricky part is that the "admin exists" and "completed" states don't have steps at all, so I'd need to conditionally render either the step indicator or just show those completion panels instead.

I'm defining a helper to compute the current step based on the ticket state—if there's no ticket yet, we're on Account; otherwise, map the ticket's step value to the corresponding index. Then I can create a method that returns the CSS classes for each step based on whether it's active, completed, or upcoming.

The "Start over" form will be placed once after the step content, conditionally shown only when we're past the account step. I also need to fix RestartAsync to properly work with the form submission handler.

Rewriting the whole Setup.razor file... ConfirmTotpAsync is marked async but doesn't actually await anything — it just calls synchronous methods like Base32Text.TryDecode and WriteTicketAndReload — which triggers a compiler warning. Since the codebase aims for zero warnings, I should either remove the async keyword and return Task.CompletedTask, or add a legitimate await somewhere. The form binding supports both async Task and non-async Task methods, so either approach works. Returning Task.CompletedTask... WriteTicketAndReload is synchronous and called from async handlers, which is fine. OnInitializedAsync properly awaits AdministratorExistsAsync without triggering CS1998. For the optional label styling, I'll just use plain text "(optional)" in the label rather than adding a new CSS class. The .link-button class should already be defined since it's used in Passkeys, so I'll apply it to the "Start over" button as a subtle text link. Now I'm ready to rewrite the Setup.razor file cleanly.

Show less
Done
The method-returning-RenderFragment templates (@<form>…) are a fragile pattern for SSR form dispatch, and ConfirmTotpAsync would trip CS1998. Let me rewrite Setup.razor with a cleaner single-panel structure, fully inline forms, and warning-free handler signatures.

I need to extract the original file contents from the dump using their line ranges, then write them to the actual repository paths so I can make precise edits. I need to figure out the exact boundaries for extracting each file's content from this structured document. The pattern is consistent: each file block has metadata followed by a content section, and files are separated by a line of 80 hashes. So for any file, I extract everything after the --- CONTENT --- marker up to (but not including) the separator line of hashes that precedes the next file header.

I'll write a helper script using awk to handle this extraction per file by computing the start and end line numbers for the content section.

The necessary using statement for the Identity namespace is already there since the test already references Person.

Since the constructor only creates a protector from IDataProtectionProvider and doesn't open a database connection, the test will pass even with the UnusedConnectionFactory that throws on connection attempts — it's purely testing that resolution works, not any database interaction.

I'm outlining two new test files: one for setup ticket validation covering round-trip behavior, tampering, expiry, and state transitions, and another for first administrator bootstrap that tests the locked commit gate. Starting with the setup ticket tests using an ephemeral data protection provider in the WebApplication.Tests project.

Writing test cases... Writing test logic... Writing test helper... So I'll use Assert.SkipWhen for the dynamic skip. For the bootstrap test, I need to wire up the connection factory, FixedClock, UuidV7IdentifierFactory, and EphemeralDataProtectionProvider, then build a DapperUserStore instance and verify the TOTP secret round-trips correctly by calling FindByNameAsync and GetAuthenticatorKeyAsync — making sure both use the same DataProtectionProvider instance.

Then I'll query the database directly to verify the person row has the right fields (username, display name, password hash, flags all set correctly), confirm the TOTP secret round-trips through the store's protector, check the passkey credential row matches what was enrolled, and validate the recovery codes.

For the recovery codes, I'll assert there are exactly 10 unused codes and that redeeming one of the returned codes succeeds (then verify the count drops to 9) to confirm single-use behavior. I'll also verify the person_role row shows administrator granted by themselves, and the security_event table contains exactly four events (account_created, passkey_registered, totp_enrolled, role_granted) with the right subjects and actors. Finally, I'll confirm the response status is Created and the returned recovery codes count is 10.

Now for the second test: I need to verify that AdministratorExistsAsync returns false initially, then true after creating the first administrator. The third test checks that calling CreateFirstAdministratorAsync again with a different pending person fails with AdministratorAlreadyExists status, leaves recovery codes empty, and doesn't create a second admin or additional person record. I'll construct a second NewAdministrator with a fresh person id and distinct username, call the method, and assert the status, empty recovery codes, and unchanged counts.

To build NewAdministrator I need a UserPasskeyInfo and a password hash—I'll use a dummy PHC-formatted string since the column accepts any text. For the TOTP secret I'll encode a fixed 20-byte value using Base32Text. Since DataAccess.Tests references DataAccess which references Domain, I have access to Base32Text and UuidV7IdentifierFactory. Now I need to construct the UserPasskeyInfo itself.

The bootstrap uses its own data protector with an internal purpose constant for the TOTP secret, so I'll need to access that same constant in the test to properly set up the encrypted secret. ose, same provider). ✓. The test never names the internal const. Both the bootstrap and store are in the same DataAccess assembly, so they can both reference the internal constant without issue. Changing from private to internal doesn't break anything since nothing external was depending on it. Now I need to write out the test files, but first I should verify whether the test assembly can actually see the bootstrap and related types like IFirstAdministratorBootstrap, DapperFirstAdministratorBootstrap, NewAdministrator, and FirstAdministratorBootstrapStatus.

They're all public in DataAccess, and DataAccess.Tests references DataAccess, so that's fine. Let me start writing SetupTicketTests.cs.
